summary: DeVault reviews Linux Mint and elementary OS as desktop Linux distributions for regular users, following up on a previous post about what desktop Linux needs. He finds elementary OS polished but incomplete, with a showstopping full disk encryption bug that would baffle average users. Linux Mint impresses him more with its comprehensive feel and reliable software ecosystem, though he criticizes its promotion of proprietary services like Netflix and its lack of organizational transparency. He concludes that Linux Mint will be his recommendation for normal users, while elementary OS has potential if it continues improving.
key_points: Both distributions handled printer setup with zero configuration, a notable achievement for desktop Linux
Elementary OS has a critical full disk encryption bug where the password prompt appears on a different TTY instead of a GUI — totally unacceptable for average users
Elementary OS is polished but too basic, with a sparse app store and apps lacking important infrequently-used features
Linux Mint feels comprehensive and robust but compromises its FOSS principles by advertising proprietary services like Netflix and YouTube in the installer
Linux Mint's organizational structure lacks transparency about leadership, user participation, and financial accountability
money_quotes: Both distros required zero printer configuration: it just worked.
This is totally unacceptable, and no average user would have any clue what to do when presented with this.
This should be a little GUI baked into the initramfs which prompts for your password on boot, and should be a regularly tested part of the installer before each elementary release ships.
Also, the default wallpaper depicts a big rock covered in bird shit, which I thought was kind of funny.
My suggestions to elementary are to consider that completeness is a kind of polish.
I was also pretty pissed to see the installer advertising nonfree, predatory services like Netflix and YouTube to me — distributions have no business advertising this kind of shit.
Linux Mint needs to be more transparent, and should also consider how its allegiance with proprietary services like Netflix acts as a long-term divestment from the FOSS ecosystem it relies on.
The system is tight, complete, and robust. Nice work.
key_insight: Completeness is itself a form of polish — a desktop Linux distribution must be comprehensive and reliable in its entirety, not just beautiful in its core experience, to serve normal users.
